summary: I first became aware of something wrong when I heard on my pocket radio that a plane had crashed into one of the Twin Towers. It was a clear, beautiful day in New York City on September 11, 2001, so I immediately thought it was an act of terrorism. I was still working on site when the second plane hit the other tower, which confirmed the attack. Watching the news coverage on TV in my office, I realized the gravity of what was happening.

On the morning of September 11, 2001, I was listening to the radio and heard that a plane had crashed into one of the Twin Towers in New York City. It was a clear day, and I immediately suspected terrorism. Although I was not in New York, the events there were the first indication that something terrible was unfolding. The news from New York set the tone for the rest of the day as we learned about further attacks.

I was at my site near Washington, D.C. when we heard and felt the crash into the Pentagon. The impact was loud enough to shake the building, which was very unusual. Shortly after, we saw smoke rising from the Pentagon area and fire trucks coming from Fort Myer. The FBI evacuated the Pentagon area fearing another attack, and many emergency workers were sent away. It was a tense and surreal experience watching the smoke and chaos unfold.

On September 11, 2001, after the Pentagon was hit, I saw fire trucks coming from Fort Myer heading toward the Pentagon. Fort Myer is close by, and during the day, I noticed people walking through the cemetery area toward Fort Myer, likely Pentagon employees trying to get home. The proximity of Fort Myer made it a key location for emergency response that day. It was part of the strange and somber atmosphere as we all tried to understand what was happening.

During the confusion on September 11, 2001, there were rumors over the radio about a car bomb at the State Department. We quickly realized these were just rumors because from where I was near Washington, D.C., there was no visible smoke or signs of an attack there. The misinformation added to the fear and uncertainty of the day. It was a reminder of how chaotic communication was during the attacks.

On the morning of September 11, 2001, there were rumors circulating that there was a fire outside the White House. From my vantage point near Washington, D.C., I could see no evidence of this, and it turned out to be false information. The rumors spread quickly and added to the tension as we tried to piece together what was really happening. It was a chaotic day with lots of uncertainty about the safety of key locations like the White House.

I was near Washington, D.C. on September 11, 2001, when the Pentagon was hit. We could see the smoke rising from the Pentagon but no other signs of attacks in the city. The rumors about other attacks in D.C., like at the State Department or White House, were unfounded. The FBI came to our site to set up a command post while scanning the skies for a possible fourth plane. The whole city was on edge, and communication was difficult that day.

On September 11, 2001, shortly after the Pentagon was hit, I saw a military transport prop plane flying very low between the ravine near here and the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier. It was an unusual sight and made me realize the seriousness of the situation. The Tomb is close to where I was working, and the low-flying plane was part of the military response that day. It was a stark reminder of the gravity of the attacks.

The FBI came to our site near Washington, D.C. on September 11, 2001, after the Pentagon was hit. They used the front of the House as a command post and were scanning the skies for a possible fourth plane. Initially, only two agents were present, but more arrived later. Their communications were poor, and they even mistakenly thought the fourth plane had hit Camp David. Later, a group of seven or eight agents came looking for the others, highlighting the confusion and chaos of the day.

On September 11, 2001, the FBI agents at our site mistakenly believed that the fourth plane had hit Camp David. This was incorrect, and my wife, who lives in Frederick County, Maryland, confirmed there was no smoke or signs of attack visible from our home. The misinformation added to the confusion and fear that day. It showed how even official sources were struggling to get accurate information during the attacks.

I live in Frederick County, Maryland, just east of the city on a hill. On September 11, 2001, I called my wife from work to check if anything was happening near Camp David because of rumors about a plane crash. She said there was nothing going on and no smoke visible from our vantage point. Living there gave me a clear view of the area and helped me realize that some of the reports we were hearing were inaccurate. It was a strange day filled with uncertainty even from home.

On the morning of September 11, 2001, I was working near Washington, D.C., when I first heard on my pocket radio that a plane had crashed into one of the Twin Towers in New York. As the day unfolded, I witnessed the Pentagon attack and the ensuing chaos, including rumors of other attacks and the FBI's response. Communication was poor, and misinformation was rampant, making the day very frightening. I stayed at the site longer than most, observing the strange atmosphere and the reactions of those around me.

I have some knowledge about aviation history, including the fact that during World War II a B-25 Mitchell bomber crashed into the Empire State Building in the 1940s. That incident happened in heavy clouds, which is very different from the clear day on September 11, 2001, when the planes hit the Twin Towers. I thought about this history when I first heard about the attacks because it showed how rare and unlikely such accidents are on clear days. It helped me immediately suspect the September 11 crashes were deliberate acts.

I know that during World War II, a B-25 Mitchell bomber crashed into the Empire State Building in the 1940s. That crash happened in heavy clouds, which contrasts with the clear skies on September 11, 2001. This piece of history came to mind when I heard about the plane crashes into the Twin Towers. It helped me understand that the September 11 attacks were not accidents but deliberate acts of terrorism.

During World War II, a B-25 Mitchell bomber crashed into the Empire State Building in the 1940s. I was familiar with this historical event and thought about it when the September 11 attacks happened. The World War II crash occurred in heavy clouds, unlike the clear day on September 11, which made me realize the latter was no accident. This historical context helped me immediately suspect terrorism when I heard about the plane hitting the Twin Towers.
